standard_description: Use punctuation to separate items in a series.
subject: Language

khan_exercises.title: Adjective order
khan_exercises.description: Put adjectives in the right order, using your new favorite memory aid, DOSA-SCOMP.
topic_path: Adjective order and commas with adjectives

items.question_content:
**Pick the best adjective order to describe the noun "car".**


[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Remember DOSA SCOMP:

Determiner, Opinion, Size, Age, Shape, Color, Origin, Material, Purpose
Following this pattern, the best adjective order is **"the new red car"**.

choices.content: the new red car
new the red car
the red car new

Determiner, Opinion, Size, Age, Shape, Color, Origin, Material, Purpose

choices.content: Colum's crumbly old stone castle

crumbly Colum's stone old castle

khan_exercises.title: Commas and adjectives
khan_exercises.description: Learn about coordinate adjectives and putting commas between them.
topic_path: Adjective order and commas with adjectives

items.question_content:
**Choose whether or not there should be a comma between the bolded adjectives.**

> They decided their **little old ** house needed to be renovated.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
There are two easy ways to tell if adjectives need a comma between them:

* Are they in the same DOSA-SCOMP category? (Determiner, Opinion, Size, Age, Shape, Color, Origin, Material, Purpose)

* Can you put "and" between the adjectives without making an awkward sentence?

If the answer to these questions is "yes", then we should place a comma between the adjectives.
"Little" and "old" are in different DOSA-SCOMP categories (size and age), so we **don't need a comma between them**.

choices.content: comma
no comma

khan_exercises.title: Commas in dialogue, tag questions, direct address, and yes-or-no responses
khan_exercises.description: Find and use commas to set off dialogue, in tag questions, for direct address, and for yes-and-no responses.
topic_path: Punctuation: end-of-sentence punctuation, the comma, and the apostrophe
Commas in dialogue, tag questions, direct address, and yes-or-no responses

items.question_content:
**Choose the correct placement for the commas in this sentence.**

>Rafael asked “Is it time to take the cookies out of the oven?”

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
When a sentence contains dialogue (in quotation marks), we need to use commas to separate the dialogue from other parts of the sentence.

For example: 

* *"This cheeseburger tastes fantastic," she said.*
* *"Are you sure," asked the sloth, "that nobody will mind if I take a nap?"*

The appropriate punctuation is as follows:

>**Rafael asked, “Is it time to take the cookies out of the oven?”**

choices.content: Rafael asked$\redD{,}$ “Is it time to take the cookies out of the oven?”
Rafael$\redD{,}$ asked “Is it time to take the cookies out of the oven?”
Rafael$\redD{,}$ asked$\redD{,}$ “Is it time to take the cookies out of the oven?”

items.question_content:
**Choose the correct placement for the commas in this sentence.**

>No I haven’t seen The Sixth Sense, and I’m not planning to.


[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
When answering a yes or no question, we often need to add extra explanation. To do so grammatically, we should separate the "yes" or "no" from the rest of the sentence with commas.

For example:

* *No, I wasn't the one who ate the last slice of pizza.*

The appropriate punctuation is as follows:

>**No, I haven’t seen The Sixth Sense, and I’m not planning to.**

choices.content: No$\redD{,}$ I haven’t seen The Sixth Sense, and I’m not planning to.
No I haven’t seen The Sixth Sense, and I’m not planning to.
No I haven’t$\redD{,}$ seen The Sixth Sense, and I’m not planning to.

items.question_content:
**Choose the correct placement for the commas in this sentence.**

>Rishi can you hand me that dish towel?

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
When we're addressing someone directly, we need to separate their name from the rest of the sentence by using commas.

For example:

* *Hey Ollie, how many brothers do you have?*
* *You should try out for the dance team, Rachel.*

The appropriate punctuation is as follows:

>**Rishi, can you hand me that dish towel?**

choices.content: Rishi can you$\redD{,}$ hand me that dish towel?
Rishi$\redD{,}$ can you hand me that dish towel?
Rishi$\redD{,}$ can you$\redD{,}$ hand me that dish towel?

items.question_content:
**Choose the correct placement for the commas in this sentence.**

>Yes you can go ahead of me in line.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
When answering a yes or no question, we often need to add extra explanation. To do so grammatically, we should separate the "yes" or "no" from the rest of the sentence with commas.

For example:

* *No, I wasn't the one who ate the last slice of pizza.*

The appropriate punctuation is as follows:

>**Yes, you can go ahead of me in line.**

choices.content: Yes you can$\redD{,}$ go ahead$\redD{,}$ of me in line.
Yes$\redD{,}$ you can go ahead of me in line.
Yes you$\redD{,}$ can go ahead of me in line.

items.question_content:
**Choose the correct placement for the commas in this sentence.**

>That tiger’s tooth looks pretty sharp doesn’t it?

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
When we add a short question to the end of a statement, we need to separate the question from the rest of the sentence by using a comma.

For example:

* *Those penguins are cute, aren't they?*

The appropriate punctuation is as follows:

>**That tiger’s tooth looks pretty sharp, doesn’t it?**

choices.content: That tiger’s tooth$\redD{,}$ looks pretty sharp doesn’t it?
That tiger’s tooth$\redD{,}$ looks pretty$\redD{,}$ sharp doesn’t it?
That tiger’s tooth looks pretty sharp$\redD{,}$ doesn’t it?

items.question_content:
**Choose the correct placement for the commas in this sentence.**

>“I’m sorry” Aina said “I don’t like blueberries.”

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
When a sentence contains dialogue (in quotation marks), we need to use commas to separate the dialogue from other parts of the sentence.

For example: 

* *"This cheeseburger tastes fantastic," she said.*
* *"Are you sure," asked the sloth, "that nobody will mind if I take a nap?"*

The appropriate punctuation is as follows:

>**“I’m sorry”, Aina said, “I don’t like blueberries.”**

choices.content: “I’m sorry” Aina said$\redD{,}$ “I don’t like$\redD{,}$ blueberries.”
“I’m sorry$\redD{,}$” Aina said$\redD{,}$ “I don’t like blueberries.”
“I’m sorry$\redD{,}$” Aina said “I don’t like blueberries.”

items.question_content:
**Choose the correct placement for the commas in this sentence.**

>You’re not going out on the ice are you?


[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
When we add a short question to the end of a statement, we need to separate the question from the rest of the sentence by using a comma.

For example:

* *Those penguins are cute, aren't they?*

The appropriate punctuation is as follows:

>**You’re not going out on the ice, are you?**

choices.content: You’re not going out on the ice$\redD{,}$ are you?
You’re not going$\redD{,}$ out on the ice are you?
You’re not going out on the ice are$\redD{,}$ you?

items.question_content:
**Choose the correct placement for the commas in this sentence.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
When a sentence contains dialogue (in quotation marks), we need to use commas to separate the dialogue from other parts of the sentence.

For example: 

* *"This cheeseburger tastes fantastic," she said.*
* *"Are you sure," asked the sloth, "that nobody will mind if I take a nap?"*

The appropriate punctuation is as follows:

khan_exercises.title: Appositives
khan_exercises.description: We use appositives to redefine or clarify a noun phrase; test your knowledge in this exercise!
topic_path: Punctuation: end-of-sentence punctuation, the comma, and the apostrophe

items.question_content:
**Choose the correct placement for the commas in this sentence.**

>My sister a fourth grade teacher has to wake up extra early to get to school before her students.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
**Appositives** are noun phrases that redefine or clarify their antecedents. An appositive might provide a name for an unnamed idea, or it might give additional details.

Appositives must be separated from the rest of the sentence by commas. An appositive at the beginning or end of a sentence only requires one comma, while one in the middle requires two.

For example:

* *Prince, **the famous rock star,** named his home Paisley Park.*
* ***A popular pizza topping,** pepperoni is a spiced sausage made from pork and beef.*

We should separate the appositive "a fourth grade teacher" from the rest of the sentence using commas:

>**My sister, a fourth grade teacher, has to wake up extra early to get to school before her students.**

choices.content: My sister$\redD{,}$ a fourth grade teacher has to wake up extra early$\redD{,}$ to get to school before her students.
My sister$\redD{,}$ a fourth grade teacher$\redD{,}$ has to wake up extra early to get to school before her students.
My sister a fourth grade teacher$\redD{,}$ has to wake up extra early$\redD{,}$ to get to school$\redD{,}$ before her students.
